input: John is standing near the tracks of a trolley. John notices that the brakes of the trolley have failed. Five workmen are working on the tracks with their backs turned. John sees that the runaway trolley is headed for the five workmen who will be killed if it proceeds on its present course. The only way to save these five workmen is to hit a switch that will turn the trolley onto the side tracks. Moreover, there is a dog on the tracks with its back turned. John knows that the five workmen and the dog will be saved if he hits the switch. John thinks 'I don't care at all about saving the dog. I just want to save the five workmen.' John decides to hit the switch. Sure enough, the trolley turns on the side tracks, the five workmen and the dog on the main tracks are saved. Did John intentionally save the dog?
debate_transcript: [Causal_Scientist]: John is standing near the tracks of a trolley. John notices that the brakes of the trolley have failed. Five workmen are working on the tracks with their backs turned. John sees that the runaway trolley is headed for the five workmen who will be killed if it proceeds on its present course. The only way to save these five workmen is to hit a switch that will turn the trolley onto the side tracks. Moreover, there is a dog on the tracks with its back turned. John knows that the five workmen and the dog will be saved if he hits the switch. John thinks 'I don't care at all about saving the dog. I just want to save the five workmen.' John decides to hit the switch. Sure enough, the trolley turns on the side tracks, the five workmen and the dog on the main tracks are saved. Did John intentionally save the dog?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: The question of whether John intentionally saved the dog seems to hinge on his stated intention to only save the five workmen. However, it's worth noting that John's decision was based on a utilitarian calculation, where he prioritized saving the greatest number of lives. This raises questions about the nature of moral agency and whether our intentions can be solely determined by our actions. Can we truly say that John 'intended' to save the dog if his primary motivation was to save the workmen? Or does his decision imply a more nuanced understanding of moral responsibility, one where the consequences of our actions take precedence over our stated intentions?

[Legal_Expert]: I think this is where things get interesting. As a legal expert, I'd argue that John's decision to hit the switch was indeed motivated by his desire to save the five workmen, but that doesn't necessarily mean he didn't have an indirect intention to save the dog as well. After all, if hitting the switch would lead to the death of the dog, wouldn't that be a consequence of his actions? In law, we often talk about 'proximate cause' - the idea that one action can lead to another, sometimes unintended, consequence. So, while John may not have explicitly intended to save the dog, it's possible that he did intend for the switch to be hit in such a way that would result in the dog being saved. But what does this say about moral agency? Is our intentionality tied so closely to our stated motivations that we can't account for the consequences of our actions?

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I think Legal_Expert makes a valid point, but I'm still not convinced that John's decision was motivated by an indirect intention to save the dog. To me, it seems like he genuinely prioritized saving the five workmen and only considered the dog as a means to achieve that end. If he had truly intended to save the dog, wouldn't he have been more concerned about its well-being? The fact that he didn't even think twice about the dog's fate suggests to me that his primary motivation was indeed to save the workmen.
